did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, and ResNet50) to classify skin lesions and detect skin cancer types from dermoscopic and clinical images. MobileNetV3 was selected as the best-performing model (80.17% accuracy on ISIC 2019) and deployed in a mobile application for early skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the evaluation of four fine-tuned deep learning models (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, and ResNet50) on specific datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, PAD-UFES-20) and the development of a mobile application for skin cancer detection. It reports new results, including accuracy metrics (e.g., 80.17% for MobileNetV3), and describes a novel technical contribution (the mobile app deployment), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Development of Mobile Application for Skin Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning Models
journal: 2024 11th International Conference on Soft Computing & Machine Intelligence
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for timely skin cancer diagnosis by developing a mobile application that leverages deep learning to classify skin lesions from dermoscopic and clinical images. The researchers evaluated four fine-tuned deep neural models—MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, and ResNet50—across three experimental settings using the HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and PAD-UFES-20 datasets. MobileNetV3 emerged as the best-performing model, achieving an accuracy of 80.17% on the ISIC 2019 dataset and 79.17% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The selected MobileNetV3 model was converted to TensorFlow Lite and integrated into a Flutter-based mobile application to provide a quick and reliable tool for early diagnosis. The results indicate that models trained on dermoscopic images can effectively classify clinical images, though with slightly lower accuracy, demonstrating the potential for broadening access to life-saving interventions.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'accurately classifying skin lesions' and 'categorizing four types of skin cancer'. The models (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, ResNet50) were evaluated using accuracy metrics on datasets with discrete classes (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, PAD-UFES-20). The output is a class label for diagnosis, not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dataset of challenging dermoscopic samples containing 10,015 images distributed among 7 lesion types with an uneven distribution.

use.training: Used for training and validation in the first experimental setting.
use.validation: Used for training and validation in the first experimental setting.
use.testing: Not tested on separately as samples are present in ISIC 2019.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A dataset composed of 25,331 dermoscopic images from several patients distributed among eight types.

use.training: Used for training and validation in the second experimental setting.
use.validation: Used for training and validation in the second experimental setting.
use.testing: Used for testing in the first and second experimental settings.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A skin dataset composed of patient data and clinical images collected from smartphones, containing 2,299 images with uneven distribution among 6 types. For the experiments, SCC and SEK classes were removed, and NV, MEL, and BCC were downsampled to 2,500 images each, while BCC and ACK were downsampled to 175 images each.

use.training: Used for training and validation in the third experimental setting (after downsampling and augmentation).
use.validation: Used for training and validation in the third experimental setting (after downsampling and augmentation).
use.testing: Used for testing in the first, second, and third experimental settings.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics.

HAM10000.notes: HAM10000 was used for training and validation in the first experiment. Separate testing was not performed on HAM10000 because its samples are present in the ISIC 2019 dataset.

ISIC 2019.notes: In the first experiment, models trained on HAM10000 were tested on ISIC 2019. In the second experiment, models trained on ISIC 2019 were tested on ISIC 2019. In the third experiment, models trained on PAD-UFES-20 were tested on ISIC 2019. MobileNetV3 achieved the highest accuracy of 80.17% in the first experiment on ISIC 2019 test set.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: In the first experiment, models trained on HAM10000 were tested on PAD-UFES-20. In the second experiment, models trained on ISIC 2019 were tested on PAD-UFES-20. In the third experiment, models trained on PAD-UFES-20 were tested on PAD-UFES-20. MobileNetV3 achieved the highest accuracy of 79.17% in the third experiment on PAD-UFES-20 test set. The paper notes that models trained on dermoscopic images could classify clinical images from PAD-UFES-20 but with lower accuracy compared to dermoscopic images in ISIC 2019.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV3 (selected as best performing), MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, ResNet50
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models (implied by 'fine-tuned' and standard practice for these architectures, though specific pretraining dataset like ImageNet is not explicitly stated in the provided text chunks, the models are standard pre-trained backbones). Note: The text mentions 'fine-tuned deep neural models'.

MobileNetV3.description: Deep neural network with efficient inverted residuals, squeeze-and-excitation (SE) blocks, and hard-swish activation functions.

MobileNetV2.description: Revised version of MobileNetV1 using depthwise separable convolutions.

EfficientNetV2.description: Uses compound scaling technique for depth, width, and resolution.

ResNet50.description: Deep neural network using residual learning.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Classification of 4 lesion types (BCC, MEL, NEV, ACK) or 7 types depending on dataset

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation for minority classes: rotation, width shift, brightness change, horizontal flip, vertical flip
Downsampling majority classes to balance dataset distribution

Augmentation operations were applied to minority training sub-directories to reach the majority count of samples in each class for the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

conclusions: MobileNetV3 was identified as the best-performing deep learning model among those tested (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, and ResNet50), achieving an accuracy of 80.17% on the ISIC 2019 dataset and 79.17% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

The study successfully developed a mobile application using Flutter and TensorFlow Lite to deploy the MobileNetV3 model for early skin cancer detection, providing a quick and reliable tool for diagnosis.

Models trained on dermoscopic images (HAM10000 and ISIC 2019) were able to classify clinical images from the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, demonstrating potential for broadening access to diagnosis, although with lower accuracy compared to dermoscopic images.

The developed mobile application aims to reduce the burden on healthcare systems and save time for patients and dermatologists by offering an accessible tool for detecting skin lesions, including healthy skin, from public data.

A limitation of the study is that the accuracy for classifying clinical images was lower than that for dermoscopic images, suggesting a need for future work to include more clinical images and potentially integrate features like patient history and appointment scheduling.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on retrospective public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, PAD-UFES-20) and lacks external validation on independent clinical cohorts or prospective trials.

There is a significant domain shift between training data (dermoscopic images) and testing data for clinical applicability (PAD-UFES-20 clinical images), resulting in lower accuracy (79.17%) compared to dermoscopic performance (80.17%).

The PAD-UFES-20 dataset was heavily manipulated for experiments: majority classes (NV, MEL, BCC) were downsampled to 2,500 images, and minority classes (BCC, ACK) were downsampled to 175 images, with oversampling via augmentation to balance classes, which may not reflect real-world prevalence.
The paper reports only accuracy as a performance metric; critical metrics for medical diagnosis such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC are not provided.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the differences in model performance.
The study excludes Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SEK) from the PAD-UFES-20 experiments, limiting the scope of the mobile application's diagnostic capabilities.
The mobile application is deployed as an offline tool using TensorFlow Lite, which limits the ability to update the model or leverage cloud-based computational resources for improved accuracy.
The HAM10000 dataset contains a severe class imbalance (e.g., 66.95% Melanocytic Nevus vs. 1.15% Dermatofibroma), which can bias model performance towards majority classes.

The paper does not address potential data leakage risks between the HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 datasets, noting that HAM10000 samples are present in ISIC 2019, though separate testing on HAM10000 was not performed.
